dataset.text: We’ve invested heavily in automation to reduce cost, but it’s actually increasing repeat contact because customers don’t trust the outcomes and agents are having to fix things afterwards.
Agents are following the process exactly as designed, but the process itself doesn’t reflect what customers actually need, so performance looks good on paper but fails in reality.
Sales is driving aggressive growth targets, but the operation hasn’t scaled capability or training, so service quality is dropping and complaints are rising.
Compliance requirements are technically being met, but agents don’t fully understand why they exist, so behaviours are inconsistent and risk is still present.
We’ve implemented new systems to support agents, but because they don’t align with how decisions are actually made in real conversations, agents ignore them and revert to old habits.
Customers are being passed between internal teams and external partners, but no one owns the end-to-end experience, so issues remain unresolved.
Partners are incentivised on volume rather than quality, which is driving behaviours that conflict with both customer outcomes and regulatory expectations.
Agents are expected to deliver personalised experiences, but the data available to them is fragmented across systems, making it impossible to act effectively in the moment.
Operational metrics show efficiency improvements, but they are masking the fact that customers are contacting more frequently to resolve the same issues.
The business has introduced AI to support decision-making, but without clear accountability or trust from agents, it is not being used in practice.
Customers cannot complete simple tasks without contacting us because the journey design still assumes internal process steps they cannot see.
The platform architecture cannot support real-time visibility across channels, so teams are making decisions on stale and fragmented information.
There is no consistent ownership model for resolving cross-functional issues, so work is repeatedly handed off and slows down delivery.
Managers are being asked to lead transformation without the coaching, confidence, or behavioural support required to do it well.
The current pricing structure rewards short-term volume growth even when it damages service quality and increases operational strain.
Key partners are critical to delivery, but their incentives, governance, and reporting lines are misaligned with the outcomes we need.
Regulatory obligations are increasing faster than our control framework is evolving, which leaves the organisation exposed despite good intentions.
